subject: English
chapter: Poems – How to Tell Wild Animals, The Ball Poem
title: Extended Reading Passages with Questions – Identity and Loss
instructions: Read each passage carefully. Answer the questions using information only from the given paragraph.

passages.paragraph: If ever you should go by chance / To jungles in the east; / And if there should to you advance / A large and tawny beast, / If he roars at you as you’re dyin’ / You’ll know it is the Asian Lion. The poet uses dark humor to suggest that the only way to truly identify the Asian Lion is through the terrifying experience of its roar during a fatal encounter.
questions: Where might one find the 'tawny beast' by chance?
What color is the 'Asian Lion' described as?
What is the final sign that confirms the animal's identity?
How would you describe the poet's tone in this identification?
Identify a word that means 'a brownish-yellow color'.

passages.paragraph: Or if some time when roaming round, / A noble wild beast greets you, / With black stripes on a yellow ground, / Just notice if he eats you. / This simple rule may help you learn / The Bengal Tiger to discern. The tiger is described as 'noble,' yet the advice remains sarcastically dangerous: if you are being eaten, you have successfully identified the Bengal Tiger.
questions: What are the physical markings of the Bengal Tiger?
What adjective does the poet use to describe the tiger?
What is the 'simple rule' for identifying this animal?
What does 'discern' mean in this context?
How is the tiger's appearance different from the lion's?

passages.paragraph: The Leopard is identified by its hide 'peppered' with spots. The poet jokes that even if you 'roar with pain,' it will do no good, because the leopard will 'only lep and lep again.' The humor comes from the play on words and the idea that the animal's natural instinct to leap is its most defining characteristic.
questions: How is the leopard's skin or hide described?
What will the leopard do if the person roars with pain?
What play on words does the poet use for the leopard's action?
Why is crying out in pain useless according to the text?
What is the primary feature used to recognize the leopard?

passages.paragraph: If when you’re walking round your yard / You meet a creature there, / Who hugs you very, very hard, / Be sure it is a Bear. If you have any doubts, the poet adds, the bear will give you 'just one more caress.' This ironic use of 'hug' and 'caress' masks the deadly strength of a bear's attack.
questions: Where might you unexpectedly meet a bear?
What is the bear's primary 'greeting'?
What will the bear do if the person is still in doubt?
Which words are used ironically to describe a bear attack?
What does this passage reveal about the bear’s physical strength?

passages.paragraph: A true Chameleon is small, a lizard-type of thing. It has no ears at all, and not a single wing. If there is nothing on the tree, 'tis the chameleon you see. This highlights the animal's ability to camouflage so perfectly that the observer sees nothing but the empty tree itself.
questions: What animal does the chameleon resemble?
What two physical features does a chameleon lack?
Why might an observer think the tree is empty?
What is the size of the chameleon?
What natural ability of the chameleon is being described?

passages.paragraph: What is the boy now, who has lost his ball, / What, what is he to do? I saw it go / Merrily bouncing, down the street, and then / Merrily over — there it is in the water! The ball, once a source of play, is suddenly gone, disappearing into the harbor and leaving the boy in a state of sudden, confusing loss.
questions: What object has the boy lost?
How did the ball move before it was lost?
Where did the ball eventually end up?
What question does the poet ask about the boy's future actions?
Identify the word used to describe the ball's joyful movement.

passages.paragraph: No use to say ‘O there are other balls’: / An ultimate shaking grief fixes the boy / As he stands rigid, trembling, staring down / All his young days into the harbour where / His ball went. The loss of the physical ball is actually the loss of the memories and childhood days associated with it.
questions: Why is it useless to tell the boy 'there are other balls'?
How does the boy's body react to the grief?
What is the boy 'staring down' into the harbor?
What does the ball represent to the boy in this passage?
What adjective is used to describe the boy's grief?

passages.paragraph: I would not intrude on him; / A dime, another ball, is worthless. Now / He senses first responsibility / In a world of possessions. The poet refuses to offer money or a replacement because the boy must learn the difficult lesson that in life, things we value will be lost, and we must learn to handle it.
questions: Why does the poet choose not to 'intrude' or help the boy?
Why is 'a dime' or 'another ball' considered worthless?
What new feeling is the boy experiencing for the first time?
How does the poet describe the world the boy lives in?
What is the 'first responsibility' mentioned in the text?

passages.paragraph: People will take balls, balls will be lost always, little boy. / And no one buys a ball back. Money is external. He is learning, well behind his desperate eyes, / The epistemology of loss, how to stand up. The boy is gaining the knowledge of how to cope with the reality that some things can never be replaced by money.
questions: What is the universal truth about 'balls' (possessions) mentioned here?
Can money buy back what is truly lost?
What is the 'epistemology of loss' referring to?
What is the ultimate lesson the boy is learning?
How are the boy's eyes described in this moment?

passages.paragraph: Knowing what every man must one day know / And most know many days, how to stand up. The poem ends with a universal realization. Every person eventually has to learn how to recover from a loss and continue living, even when they have lost something that was precious to them.
questions: According to the text, what must every man eventually know?
What is the phrase 'how to stand up' a metaphor for?
Is the experience of loss rare or common according to the poet?
What is the boy doing at the end of the poem?
Summarize the final message of the passage.
